is a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") located in [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the borough's population was 12,550,[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)[[10]](#cite_note-LWD2020-10) an increase of 957 (+8.3%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 11,593,[[19]](#cite_note-Census2010-19)[[20]](#cite_note-LWD2010-20) which in turn reflected a decline of 66 (−0.6%) from the 11,659 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

**Haddonfield** is a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") located in [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the borough's population was 12,550,[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)[[10]](#cite_note-LWD2020-10) an increase of 957 (+8.3%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 11,593,[[19]](#cite_note-Census2010-19)[[20]](#cite_note-LWD2010-20) which in turn reflected a decline of 66 (−0.6%) from the 11,659 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Haddonfield was incorporated by an act of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") on April 6, 1875, within portions of [Haddon Township](/wiki/Haddon_Township,_New_Jersey "Haddon Township, New Jersey") following a referendum on the same day. The borough became an independent municipality in 1894.[[22]](#cite_note-Story-22) The borough was named for [Elizabeth Haddon](/wiki/Elizabeth_Haddon "Elizabeth Haddon"), an early settler of the area.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") were indigenous to Haddonfield and the surrounding area, but this [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribe largely disappeared following European arrival. [Arrowheads](/wiki/Arrowhead "Arrowhead") and [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery") shards have been found by the banks of the [Cooper River](/wiki/Cooper_River_(New_Jersey) "Cooper River (New Jersey)"). The Lenape heritage of the borough is marked with a monument marking their connection to the area.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Hadrosaurus_Foulkii.JPG/250px-Hadrosaurus_Foulkii.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hadrosaurus_Foulkii.JPG)

Plaques at the [Hadrosaurus Foulkii Leidy Site](/wiki/Hadrosaurus_Foulkii_Leidy_Site "Hadrosaurus Foulkii Leidy Site") showing National Historic Landmark status (left) and a plaque from Philadelphia's [Academy of Natural Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Natural_Sciences_of_Philadelphia "Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia"). At right, toy dinosaurs have been left by visitors. A deep pit or ravine is straight ahead about 10 yards

On October 23, 1682, Francis Collins, an English [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") and a bricklayer by trade, became the first settler within the boundaries of what today is Haddonfield. Collins soon built a house, "Mountwell," on a tract of 400 acres (160 ha). Haddonfield was further developed by [Elizabeth Haddon](/wiki/Elizabeth_Haddon "Elizabeth Haddon") (1680–1762), whose Quaker father, John Haddon, bought a 500 acres (2.0 km2) tract of land in the English colony of [West Jersey](/wiki/West_Jersey "West Jersey") to escape religious persecution. Elizabeth set sail alone from [Southwark](/wiki/Southwark "Southwark"), England to the New World in 1701. Shortly after her arrival, she made a marriage proposal to [John Estaugh](/wiki/John_Estaugh "John Estaugh"), a Quaker minister, and they were married in 1702. The town was named for John Haddon, though he never came to America.[[26]](#cite_note-WP-4Nov09-26)

The [Indian King Tavern](/wiki/Indian_King_Tavern "Indian King Tavern"), built in 1750, played a significant role in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). During that war, the New Jersey Legislature met there, avoiding British forces, and in 1777, declared New Jersey to be an independent state. Today the tavern is a state historical site and museum.[[26]](#cite_note-WP-4Nov09-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Haddonfield_Quaker-27) Nevertheless, since 1873, Haddonfield has been a [dry town](/wiki/Dry_county "Dry county") where alcohol cannot be sold though it can be brewed and distributed in town.[[27]](#cite_note-Haddonfield_Quaker-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Haddonfield is a significant historic [paleontology](/wiki/Paleontology "Paleontology") site. In 1838, William Estaugh Hopkins uncovered large bones in a [marl](/wiki/Marl "Marl") pit in which he was digging. Hopkins displayed the bones at his home, Birdwood; and these bones sparked the interest of a visitor, [William Foulke](/wiki/William_Parker_Foulke "William Parker Foulke"). In 1858, Foulke dug from the marl pit the first relatively complete skeleton of a [dinosaur](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur") found in North America, *[Hadrosaurus](/wiki/Hadrosaurus "Hadrosaurus") foulkii*. The skeleton was assembled in 1868 and is still displayed at [Philadelphia Academy of Natural Sciences](/wiki/Philadelphia_Academy_of_Natural_Sciences "Philadelphia Academy of Natural Sciences").[[30]](#cite_note-30) A 17-foot (5.2 m) long bronze sculpture of “Haddy”, by sculptor John Giannotti, stands in the center of town.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[26]](#cite_note-WP-4Nov09-26) Hadrosaurus was recognized officially as the [state dinosaur of New Jersey](/wiki/List_of_New_Jersey_state_symbols "List of New Jersey state symbols") in June 1991.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In 1875, Haddonfield became the first community to secede from [Haddon Township](/wiki/Haddon_Township,_New_Jersey "Haddon Township, New Jersey") and become a self-governing borough.[[22]](#cite_note-Story-22) Haddonfield is noted for its historic homes, quaint shops, and legions of lawyers. As a legal center for southern New Jersey, the town houses the offices of more than 390 attorneys.

Haddonfield once was home to Symphony in C (formerly the Haddonfield Symphony), which is now based in nearby [Collingswood](/wiki/Collingswood,_New_Jersey "Collingswood, New Jersey"), and performs at the Gordon Theater at [Rutgers University-Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_University-Camden "Rutgers University-Camden").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Haddonfield is home to the second oldest volunteer fire company in continuous service in the United States. Haddon Fire Company No. 1 was established as Friendship Fire Company on March 8, 1764, by 26 townsmen. Each member was to furnish two leather buckets while the company supplied six ladders and three [fire hooks](/wiki/Pike_pole "Pike pole").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In 1971, Haddonfield became the second municipality in New Jersey (after [Cape May](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey")) to establish a historic preservation district.[[35]](#cite_note-35) In keeping with the historic appearance of the borough, some candidates for commissioner distribute colored ribbons to their supporters instead of yard signs.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the borough had a total area of 2.84 square miles (7.36 km2), including 2.80 square miles (7.24 km2) of land and 0.05 square miles (0.12 km2) of water (1.58%).[[1]](#cite_note-CensusArea-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GR1-2)

The [Cooper River](/wiki/Cooper_River_(New_Jersey) "Cooper River (New Jersey)") forms the border between Haddonfield and [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey"). Haddonfield shares land borders with [Audubon](/wiki/Audubon,_New_Jersey "Audubon, New Jersey"), [Barrington](/wiki/Barrington,_New_Jersey "Barrington, New Jersey"), [Haddon Township](/wiki/Haddon_Township,_New_Jersey "Haddon Township, New Jersey"), [Haddon Heights](/wiki/Haddon_Heights,_New_Jersey "Haddon Heights, New Jersey"), [Lawnside](/wiki/Lawnside,_New_Jersey "Lawnside, New Jersey"), and [Tavistock](/wiki/Tavistock,_New_Jersey "Tavistock, New Jersey").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Bodies of water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Bodies of water")]

* Hopkins Pond, covering 33 acres (13 ha), is contained by a large earthen dam, and Hopkins Lane is built atop it. The pond was created in 1789, when John Estaugh Hopkins built a dam on a tributary of the Cooper River to power a gristmill that operated until the 1850s.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In recent years, local officials have raised concerns about the pond being contaminated with [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria") that can produce toxic algae blooms.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* Driscoll Pond, contained by a small wooden dam, is below Hopkins Pond, and Hopkins Pond flows into it. Driscoll Pond is part of the Hopkins Pond park.
* Evans Pond is part of Wallworth Park and located above Wallworth Lake with a dam separating the two. Formerly Evans Pond was deep enough for small boats to sail on it.
* Wallworth Lake, in Wallworth Park, is below Evans Pond, and contained by another dam.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") counted 11,593 people, 4,436 households, and 3,181 families in the borough. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 4,104.9 per square mile (1,584.9/km2). There were 4,634 housing units at an average density of 1,640.8 per square mile (633.5/km2). The racial makeup was 95.23% (11,040) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._census) "White (U.S. census)"), 1.11% (129) [Black or African American](/wiki/Black_(U.S._census) "Black (U.S. census)"), 0.03% (4) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._census) "Native American (U.S. census)"), 1.85% (215) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._census) "Asian (U.S. census)"), 0.00% (0) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. census)"), 0.43% (50) from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 1.34% (155) from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._census) "Hispanic (U.S. census)") of any race were 2.14% (248) of the population.[[19]](#cite_note-Census2010-19)

Of the 4,436 households, 36.0% had children under the age of 18; 61.7% were married couples living together; 7.8% had a female householder with no husband present and 28.3% were non-families. Of all households, 24.9% were made up of individuals and 11.9% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.61 and the average family size was 3.17.[[19]](#cite_note-Census2010-19)

27.7% of the population were under the age of 18, 5.3% from 18 to 24, 20.4% from 25 to 44, 31.6% from 45 to 64, and 15.0% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 42.9 years. For every 100 females, the population had 91.3 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older there were 88.7 males.[[19]](#cite_note-Census2010-19)

The Census Bureau's 2006–2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") showed that (in 2010 [inflation-adjusted](/wiki/Inflation_adjustment "Inflation adjustment") dollars) [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $112,105 (with a margin of error of +/− $10,416) and the median family income was $129,100 (+/− $16,987). Males had a median income of $92,409 (+/− $10,521) versus $61,272 (+/− $6,669) for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the borough was $55,955 (+/− $5,275). About 3.8% of families and 4.9% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 4.5% of those under age 18 and 7.2% of those age 65 or over.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Notable locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Notable locations")]

The [Indian King Tavern](/wiki/Indian_King_Tavern "Indian King Tavern") was a colonial American tavern where, in 1777, the [New Jersey General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") held a meeting at which they officially declared New Jersey to be an independent state. It has since been declared a State Historic Site, restored to its original layout, and turned into a museum with guided tours available to the public.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

On the "[Main Street](/wiki/Main_Street "Main Street")" of Haddonfield, Kings Highway, there is a statue of a [Hadrosaurus](/wiki/Hadrosaurus "Hadrosaurus"), a type of [dinosaur](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur") discovered in Haddonfield. The statue, unveiled in October 2003, has been described as "the central landmark of downtown Haddonfield." The statue serves as a mascot of sorts for Haddonfield.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

Haddonfield has several parks maintained by the Camden County Parks Department:

* [Hopkins Pond](/wiki/Hopkins_Pond_(park) "Hopkins Pond (park)") covers 33.10 acres (13.40 ha) and contains both Hopkins Pond and Driscoll Pond.[[56]](#cite_note-56)
* Pennypacker Park contains the [Hadrosaurus Foulkii Leidy Site](/wiki/Hadrosaurus_Foulkii_Leidy_Site "Hadrosaurus Foulkii Leidy Site") and is near the Cooper River.[[57]](#cite_note-57)
* Wallworth Park contains Evans Pond and Wallworth Pond. Evans Pond is dammed and flows into Wallworth Pond, which is also dammed. Each of these ponds is actually a section of the [Cooper River](/wiki/Cooper_River_(New_Jersey) "Cooper River (New Jersey)"), and the early headwaters of the Cooper flow into Evans Pond.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

It also has several parks maintained by other groups:

* The Crows Woods Complex contains community gardens,[[59]](#cite_note-59) fields for public use, and a hiking loop.[[60]](#cite_note-60)
* Mountwell Park contains a small playground and a baseball field along with wooded areas.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Government")]

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Local government")]

The Borough of Haddonfield has been governed under the [Walsh Act](/wiki/Walsh_Act "Walsh Act") since 1913.[[6]](#cite_note-DataBook-6)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) The borough is one of 30 municipalities (of the 564) statewide that use the [commission form of government](/wiki/City_commission_government "City commission government").[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) The governing body is comprised of three commissioners, who elected to concurrent four-year terms of office on a [non-partisan](/wiki/Non-partisan_democracy "Non-partisan democracy") basis as part of the May municipal elections. At a reorganization meeting held after the new council is seated, each Commissioner is assigned to oversee one of the three departments within the Borough and the Commissioners select a Mayor and may select a Deputy Mayor.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the borough's commissioners are
[Mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") Dave Siedell (Commissioner of Revenue and Finance),
Itir Cole (Commissioner of Public Works, Parks and Public Property) and
Frank Troy (Commissioner of Public Affairs and Public Safety), all of whom are serving concurrent terms of office ending May 15, 2029.[[3]](#cite_note-Commission-3)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-Camden2025Municipal-68)

In 2018, the borough had an average property tax bill of $15,182, the highest in the county (though the mini municipality of [Tavistock](/wiki/Tavistock,_New_Jersey "Tavistock, New Jersey") had an average bill of $31,376 for its three homes), compared to an average bill of $8,767 statewide.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

#### Borough Hall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Borough Hall")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Haddonfield_Muni_Hall_NJ.JPG/250px-Haddonfield_Muni_Hall_NJ.JPG)](/wiki/File:Haddonfield_Muni_Hall_NJ.JPG)

Haddonfield Borough Hall

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Githens_Shop_Haddonfield.JPG/250px-Githens_Shop_Haddonfield.JPG)](/wiki/File:Githens_Shop_Haddonfield.JPG)

Githen's Shop c. 1830 in the Haddonfield Historic District.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Haddon_Fortnightly.JPG/250px-Haddon_Fortnightly.JPG)](/wiki/File:Haddon_Fortnightly.JPG)

[Haddon Fortnightly Club House](/wiki/Haddon_Fortnightly_Club_House "Haddon Fortnightly Club House") on Kings Highway.

Borough Hall, the home of Haddonfield government, is located at 242 Kings Highway East and was built in 1928 by Walter William Sharpley. There are four main offices, including those for the tax assessor, the construction office and the municipal court office. Borough Hall includes a police department, a courtroom, and an auditorium. Its walls are of marble, steel, or plaster, although police station main walls are of steel and cinder block. From May through August 2021, the Haddonfield Police issued an average of 395 tickets per month.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In Borough Hall's auditorium are paintings of men who signed the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") from New Jersey: [Abraham Clark](/wiki/Abraham_Clark "Abraham Clark"), [Francis Hopkinson](/wiki/Francis_Hopkinson "Francis Hopkinson"), [Richard Stockton](/wiki/Richard_Stockton_(Continental_Congressman) "Richard Stockton (Continental Congressman)"), and [John Witherspoon](/wiki/John_Witherspoon "John Witherspoon"). Some of the paintings are original, other copies.

Weddings have been held in Borough Hall, and while asbestos was being removed from the public library, the upper level of Borough Hall became a temporary library.

Regular meetings of the Board of Commissioners are held twice each month, usually on Mondays, in the Borough Hall auditorium: A work session at 6:30pm at the beginning of the month and an action meeting at 7:30pm the end of the month. The dates are posted on the Borough's website.

### Federal, state and county representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Federal, state and county representation")]

Haddonfield is located in the 1st Congressional District[[73]](#cite_note-PCR2012-73) and is part of New Jersey's 6th state legislative district.[[74]](#cite_note-Districts2011-74)[[75]](#cite_note-LWV2019-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

For the [119th United States Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), [New Jersey's 1st congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_1st_congressional_district "New Jersey's 1st congressional district") is represented by [Donald Norcross](/wiki/Donald_Norcross "Donald Norcross") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey")).[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) New Jersey is represented in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") by [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Cory Booker](/wiki/Cory_Booker "Cory Booker") ([Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), term ends 2027) and [Andy Kim](/wiki/Andy_Kim "Andy Kim") ([Moorestown](/wiki/Moorestown,_New_Jersey "Moorestown, New Jersey"), term ends 2031).[[79]](#cite_note-79)

For the [2026–2027 session](/wiki/222nd_New_Jersey_Legislature "222nd New Jersey Legislature"), the [6th legislative district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_6th_legislative_district "New Jersey's 6th legislative district") of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") is represented in the [State Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_Senate "New Jersey Senate") by [James Beach](/wiki/James_Beach "James Beach") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), [Voorhees Township](/wiki/Voorhees_Township,_New_Jersey "Voorhees Township, New Jersey")) and in the [General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") by [Louis Greenwald](/wiki/Louis_Greenwald "Louis Greenwald") (D, Voorhees Township) and [Melinda Kane](/wiki/Melinda_Kane "Melinda Kane") (D, [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey")).[[80]](#cite_note-80)

[Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey") is governed by a [Board of County Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_County_Commissioners_(New_Jersey) "Board of County Commissioners (New Jersey)") composed of seven members chosen [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") in partisan elections for three-year terms on a staggered basis by the residents of the county, with either two or three seats up for election each year as part of the November general election. At a reorganization meeting held in January after each election, the newly constituted Board of Commissioners selects one member to serve as Director and another as Deputy Director, each serving a one-year term in that role.[[81]](#cite_note-About-81) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit), Camden County's Commissioners are:

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Education")]

### Public schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Public schools")]

The [Haddonfield Public Schools](/wiki/Haddonfield_Public_Schools "Haddonfield Public Schools") is a comprehensive public school district serving students in [pre-kindergarten](/wiki/Pre-kindergarten "Pre-kindergarten") through [twelfth grade](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade").[[111]](#cite_note-111) The district serves students from Haddonfield, along with those from [Tavistock](/wiki/Tavistock,_New_Jersey "Tavistock, New Jersey") who attend the district's schools as part of [sending/receiving relationships](/wiki/Sending/receiving_relationship "Sending/receiving relationship").[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) As of the 2022–23 school year, the district, comprised of five schools, had an enrollment of 2,766 students and 209.5 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 13.2:1.[[115]](#cite_note-NCES-115) Schools in the district (with 2022–23 enrollment data from the [National Center for Education Statistics](/wiki/National_Center_for_Education_Statistics "National Center for Education Statistics")[[116]](#cite_note-116)) are
Central Elementary School[[117]](#cite_note-117) with 437 students in grades PreK-5,
Elizabeth Haddon Elementary School[[118]](#cite_note-118) with 376 students in grades PreK-5,
J. Fithian Tatem Elementary School[[119]](#cite_note-119) with 432 students in grades PreK-5,
Haddonfield Middle School[[120]](#cite_note-120) with 647 students in grades 6-8 and
[Haddonfield Memorial High School](/wiki/Haddonfield_Memorial_High_School "Haddonfield Memorial High School")[[121]](#cite_note-121) with 866 students in grades 9-12.[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123)

In 2023, J. Fithian Tatem Elementary School was one of nine schools in New Jersey that was recognized as a [National Blue Ribbon School](/wiki/National_Blue_Ribbon_Schools_Program "National Blue Ribbon Schools Program") by the [United States Department of Education](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education "United States Department of Education").[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125) In 2015, Elizabeth Haddon School was one of 15 schools in the state and one of nine public schools, recognized as a National Blue Ribbon School in the exemplary high performing category.[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127) Haddonfield Memorial High School was also awarded the National Blue Ribbon School Award of Excellence in 2004-05 by the United States Department of Education, the highest award an American school can receive.[[128]](#cite_note-128) In 2023, J. Fithian Tatem Elementary School became the third Haddonfield public school to receive the award.[[129]](#cite_note-129) Additionally, Haddonfield Memorial High School was ranked 33rd in public high school in New Jersey out of 328 schools statewide in *[New Jersey Monthly](/wiki/New_Jersey_Monthly "New Jersey Monthly")* magazine's September 2012 cover story on the state's "Top Public High Schools", after being ranked 11th in 2010 out of 322 schools listed.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

### Private schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Private schools")]

[Haddonfield Friends School](/wiki/Haddonfield_Friends_School "Haddonfield Friends School"), a [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") school that dates back to 1786, served 167 students in Pre-K through eighth grade.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Kingsway Learning Center provides [special education](/wiki/Special_education "Special education") for students from ages birth to 14 at the Haddonfield campus, which is home to the school's Early Intervention Program and its Elementary Program.[[133]](#cite_note-133) The school closed down in 2019, and was purchased by the Haddonfield Board of Education in February 2023 for $1.495 million.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) The district plans on converting the building into a fullday kindergarten and preschool by 2025.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

Christ the King Regional School, founded in 1940, serves students in Pre-K3 through eighth grade and operates under the auspices of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Camden "Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden").[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

Bancroft School, founded in Haddonfield in 1883 and located there until 2017, is special education school and neurobehavioral stabilization program. In July 2005, Bancroft began soliciting requests for proposals to purchase its 20-acre (81,000 m2) property, as a precursor to moving from Haddonfield.[[139]](#cite_note-139) Bancroft is now located in neighboring Mount Laurel, but during the late 2010s, redevelopment of the Bancroft property in Haddonfield became a locally contentious issue and remains to be resolved.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

The Beechwood School, founded in 1951, is a private pre-school educating children 2.5 through 6 years old.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

## Special events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Special events")]

There are events such as the community sidewalk sale in the summer, and the fall festival in October. The fall festival is an event where community organizations may have booths along Kings Highway and there is scarecrow-making for kids. Haddonfield hosts a weekly [farmers' market](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market") on Saturdays from May to November. There is also the Haddonfield Crafts & Fine Arts Festival, where a large variety of vendors line the main street. Another event is [First Night](/wiki/First_Night "First Night"), a [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve") celebration of the arts, with a variety of performances was held in town until 2016.[[142]](#cite_note-142) There is also a yearly car show that takes place during the second Saturday of September. There are also events such as historic house tours and designer show houses.

There is also an annual skirmish that takes place on Kings Highway East in June every year, hosted by the [Haddonfield Historical Society](/wiki/Indian_King_Tavern "Indian King Tavern"). Residents of Haddonfield dress as [redcoats](/wiki/Red_coat_(military_uniform) "Red coat (military uniform)") and [militia men](/wiki/Minutemen "Minutemen"), reenacting the battle that took place as the British retreated from the [Battle of Red Bank](/wiki/Battle_of_Red_Bank "Battle of Red Bank").[[143]](#cite_note-143) Their efforts to "annoy" the redcoats[[144]](#cite_note-144) worked as [Gen. Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") defeated the British army at the [Battle of Monmouth](/wiki/Battle_of_Monmouth "Battle of Monmouth") a month later.[[145]](#cite_note-145) The event includes many additional activities such as a yelling contest, colonial dancing, pillory prisoners, character reenactors, and tours of [Historic Haddonfield](/wiki/Haddonfield_Historic_District "Haddonfield Historic District").[[146]](#cite_note-146)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Haddonfield prides itself on being very walkable; most streets have sidewalks, and due to the small size of the town—2 miles (3.2 km) or less from any point in Haddonfield to any other as the crow flies—it is possible to walk to any part of the community. The Borough presently has a traffic campaign using the slogan "Haddonfield Drives 25" promoting the borough's speed limit as 25 mph (40 km/h) for all streets and roadways.

### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

The southbound [New Jersey Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike") in Haddonfield

As of May 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the borough had a total of 46.74 miles (75.22 km) of roadways, of which 37.67 miles (60.62 km) were maintained by the municipality, 8.85 miles (14.24 km) by Camden County, 0.09 miles (0.14 km) by the [New Jersey Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Transportation "New Jersey Department of Transportation"), and 0.13 miles (0.21 km) by the [New Jersey Turnpike Authority](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike_Authority "New Jersey Turnpike Authority").[[147]](#cite_note-147)

[Route 41](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_41 "New Jersey Route 41") (Kings Highway) passes through the center of the borough[[148]](#cite_note-148) and intersects [County Route 561](/wiki/County_Route_561_(New_Jersey) "County Route 561 (New Jersey)") (Haddon Avenue) at Haddonfield's main business district.[[149]](#cite_note-149) [Interstate 295](/wiki/Interstate_295_(Delaware%E2%80%93Pennsylvania) "Interstate 295 (Delaware–Pennsylvania)") is adjacent to the southern tip with Exit 31 straddling the border.[[150]](#cite_note-150) The [New Jersey Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike") briefly crosses through the borough, but the closest exit is Interchange 3 in [Bellmawr](/wiki/Bellmawr,_New_Jersey "Bellmawr, New Jersey") / [Runnemede](/wiki/Runnemede,_New_Jersey "Runnemede, New Jersey").[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152)

### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/NJT_5166_on_Atlantic_City_Line_train_passing_Haddonfield_station.jpg/250px-NJT_5166_on_Atlantic_City_Line_train_passing_Haddonfield_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:NJT_5166_on_Atlantic_City_Line_train_passing_Haddonfield_station.jpg)

Train at [Haddonfield station](/wiki/Haddonfield_station "Haddonfield station")

The [PATCO Speedline](/wiki/PATCO_Speedline "PATCO Speedline") [Haddonfield station](/wiki/Haddonfield_station "Haddonfield station") links it to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), in the west and to the eastern terminus in [Lindenwold, New Jersey](/wiki/Lindenwold,_New_Jersey "Lindenwold, New Jersey"), where it is possible to transfer to NJ Transit's bus and rail routes connecting Philadelphia to [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey").

[NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") provides local bus service; its [451](/wiki/451_(New_Jersey_bus) "451 (New Jersey bus)"), [455](/wiki/455_(New_Jersey_bus) "455 (New Jersey bus)"), and [457](/wiki/457_(New_Jersey_bus) "457 (New Jersey bus)") routes all stop at the PATCO station.[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Haddonfield,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

* In the film *[When Harry Met Sally...](/wiki/When_Harry_Met_Sally... "When Harry Met Sally...")* (directed by [Rob Reiner](/wiki/Rob_Reiner "Rob Reiner")), [Billy Crystal](/wiki/Billy_Crystal "Billy Crystal")'s character, Harry, is from Haddonfield.[[155]](#cite_note-155)
* Several films in the *[Halloween](/wiki/Halloween_(franchise) "Halloween (franchise)")* franchise are set in fictional Haddonfield, Illinois which was inspired by Haddonfield, N.J. [Debra Hill](/wiki/Debra_Hill "Debra Hill"), the co-writer of [the original film](/wiki/Halloween_(1978_film) "Halloween (1978 film)"), grew up in Haddonfield, New Jersey.[[156]](#cite_note-Hill-156)
* A scene in the film *[AI](/wiki/AI_(film) "AI (film)")* takes place in Haddonfield and captures a shot of a house on Kings Highway. This is the location of the Flesh Fair, a rally of anti-robot activists.[[157]](#cite_note-157)
* Photographer [Frank Stefanko](/wiki/Frank_Stefanko "Frank Stefanko") took two famous album covers for [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen") in Haddonfield: *[Darkness on the Edge of Town](/wiki/Darkness_on_the_Edge_of_Town "Darkness on the Edge of Town")* (1978) and *[The River](/wiki/The_River_(Bruce_Springsteen_album) "The River (Bruce Springsteen album)")* (1980).[[158]](#cite_note-SanDiego-158)

People who were born in, residents of, or otherwise closely associated with Haddonfield include:

* [John Adler](/wiki/John_Adler "John Adler") (1959–2011), politician who served as the U.S. representative for [New Jersey's 3rd congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_3rd_congressional_district "New Jersey's 3rd congressional district") from 2009 until 2011[[159]](#cite_note-159)
* [Graham Alexander](/wiki/Graham_Alexander_(musician) "Graham Alexander (musician)") (born 1989), singer-songwriter, entertainer, and entrepreneur known for the Broadway shows *[Rain: A Tribute to the Beatles](/wiki/Rain:_A_Tribute_to_the_Beatles "Rain: A Tribute to the Beatles")* and *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(musical) "Let It Be (musical)")* and as the founder of a new incarnation of the [Victor Talking Machine Co.](/wiki/Victor_Talking_Machine_Co. "Victor Talking Machine Co.")[[160]](#cite_note-160)
* [Abraham Anderson](/wiki/Abraham_Anderson "Abraham Anderson") (1829–1915), businessman who was a co-founder of the [Campbell Soup Company](/wiki/Campbell_Soup_Company "Campbell Soup Company")[[161]](#cite_note-161)
* [Chris Barrett](/wiki/Chris_Barrett_(filmmaker) "Chris Barrett (filmmaker)") (born 1982), filmmaker[[162]](#cite_note-162)
* [Curtis Bashaw](/wiki/Curtis_Bashaw_(politician) "Curtis Bashaw (politician)") (born 1960), real estate developer, entrepreneur and businessman, [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") nominee for the [2024 United States Senate election in New Jersey](/wiki/2024_United_States_Senate_election_in_New_Jersey "2024 United States Senate election in New Jersey")[[163]](#cite_note-163)
* [George Batten](/wiki/George_Batten_(baseball) "George Batten (baseball)") (1891–1972), [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman") who played in a single MLB game, for the [New York Highlanders](/wiki/New_York_Highlanders "New York Highlanders")[[164]](#cite_note-164)
* [Aimee Belgard](/wiki/Aimee_Belgard "Aimee Belgard") (born 1974), lawyer and politician who serves as a judge in [New Jersey Superior Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Superior_Court "New Jersey Superior Court")[[165]](#cite_note-165)
* [Brian Boucher](/wiki/Brian_Boucher "Brian Boucher") (born 1977), [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") goalie[[166]](#cite_note-JerseyMan-166)
* [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") (born 1987), former [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") winner who was quarterback for 4 NFL teams from 2010 to 2018 including the longest stint being with St. Louis Rams[[167]](#cite_note-NJ2015-167)
* [Andy Breckman](/wiki/Andy_Breckman "Andy Breckman") (born 1955), film and television writer whose work includes *[Monk](/wiki/Monk_(TV_series) "Monk (TV series)")*[[168]](#cite_note-168)
* [Daniel Brière](/wiki/Daniel_Bri%C3%A8re "Daniel Brière") (born 1977), NHL player[[166]](#cite_note-JerseyMan-166)[[169]](#cite_note-169)
* [Alexander Oswald Brodie](/wiki/Alexander_Oswald_Brodie "Alexander Oswald Brodie") (1849–1918), military officer and engineer who was appointed as Governor of [Arizona Territory](/wiki/Arizona_Territory "Arizona Territory") from 1902 to 1905[[170]](#cite_note-170)
* [Robert Byrd](/wiki/Robert_Byrd_(artist) "Robert Byrd (artist)") (born 1942), author and illustrator[[171]](#cite_note-171)
* [William T. Cahill](/wiki/William_T._Cahill "William T. Cahill") (1912–1996), [Governor of New Jersey](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey") (1970–1974)[[172]](#cite_note-172)

* [James A. Corea](/wiki/James_A._Corea "James A. Corea") (1937–2001), radio personality and specialist in nutrition, rehabilitation and sports medicine[[176]](#cite_note-176)

Dickey") (1920–2008), politician who served as Speaker of the [New Jersey General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") and as chairman of the [Delaware River Port Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_Port_Authority "Delaware River Port Authority")[[177]](#cite_note-177)
* [Greg Dobbs](/wiki/Greg_Dobbs "Greg Dobbs") (born 1978), [MLB](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") player who played for the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies")[[166]](#cite_note-JerseyMan-166)
